response:
In C# it is possible to measure the time taken for a UDP packet to reach a server  
but it is not straightforward  
UDP does not guarantee delivery or timing information  
To estimate the round trip time you can use the Ping class  
However this measures the round trip time not just one direction  
To get one way time you would need to send a packet and receive a reply  
This is not supported by standard UDP  
You can use the Stopwatch class to measure the time between sending and receiving  
But this requires the server to respond  
If the server does not respond you cannot measure the one way time  
You can also use the System.Net.NetworkInformation.Ping class  
But again this provides round trip time  
To get one way time you would need to implement your own protocol  
This involves sending a packet and waiting for a response  
You can use the UdpClient class to send and receive packets  
But you must handle the timing yourself  
You can start a Stopwatch before sending the packet  
And stop it when you receive the response  
This will give you the time taken for the packet to reach the server and return  
If the server does not respond the time will be unknown  
You can also use the Send and Receive methods of UdpClient  
To send and receive packets  
You can use the GetTimestamp and GetTimestampOffset methods  
But these are not available in all versions of .NET  
You can also use the IPStatistics class to get information about the network  
But this does not provide the time taken for a single packet to reach the server  
In summary measuring the one way time for a UDP packet is not supported by standard libraries  
You must implement your own solution using UdpClient and Stopwatch  
This requires handling the timing manually and waiting for a response  
If the server does not respond the time will be unknown  
You can also use the Ping class to get an estimate of the round trip time  
But this is not the same as one way time  
You can use the PingOptions class to set the timeout and other options  
But again this provides round trip time not one way time  
You can also use the System.Net.Sockets.UdpClient class  
To send and receive packets and measure the time manually  
This is the most reliable way to get the one way time  
But it requires handling the timing yourself and waiting for a response
